title: Gujarati Poha Khichdi

subs.name: 2 cups thin semolina (fine rava) — turns the dish into a variation of upma-khichdi

ingredients.name: potato, boiled and diced into small cubes

subs.name: skip and add extra peas for a lighter version, or use diced boiled sweet potato for a naturally sweet twist
roasted cashew halves for a richer version; or skip entirely for a nut-free dish

ingredients.name: sugar
visual_checks: Gujarati touch of sweetness; do not skip
ingredients.notes: Gujarati touch of sweetness; do not skip

steps.text: Rinse the poha in a colander under cold water for 30 seconds, toss and drain until just moist. Sprinkle turmeric, sugar and a pinch of salt over the drained poha and toss gently to colour it evenly.

Heat oil in a kadai on medium-high. Add mustard seeds and wait for them to pop. Add sesame seeds (if using), asafoetida and curry leaves; fry for 10 seconds. Add green chilli and ginger; stir for 20 seconds.
Add the peas and sauté for 1 minute until heated through. Add the diced boiled potato and roasted peanuts; toss gently for 30 seconds to warm everything without breaking the potato pieces.

Reduce heat to low and add the seasoned poha. Fold everything together with a wide spatula using gentle lifting movements — do not stir hard or the poha grains will become a paste. Every grain should be coated in the turmeric-oil.

Cover and cook on low for 2 minutes so the poha steams through and turns tender. Remove from heat, drizzle lemon juice, adjust salt and fold once more. Serve immediately with a cup of chai and scatter coriander leaves on top.

substitution_notes: poha → 2 cups thin semolina (fine rava) — turns the dish into a variation of upma-khichdi; peanut → roasted cashew halves for a richer version; or skip entirely for a nut-free dish; potato → skip and add extra peas for a lighter version, or use diced boiled sweet potato for a naturally sweet twist
